glossary.def: A distance function satisfying four axioms: non-negativity, identity of indiscernibles (zero distance means the same point), symmetry, and the triangle inequality. Euclidean, Manhattan, Mahalanobis and Jaccard distance are metrics; cosine and squared-Euclidean distance are not.
A family of distances d_p = (Σ|xᵢ−yᵢ|^p)^(1/p) controlled by an order p. p=1 gives Manhattan, p=2 gives Euclidean, and p→∞ gives Chebyshev (the largest single coordinate gap).
The Minkowski distance of order 1: the sum of absolute coordinate differences, as if walking along a city grid. More robust to outliers than Euclidean and concentrates more slowly in high dimensions.

Distance measured in units of the data's own spread: d_M = √((x−μ)ᵀ Σ⁻¹ (x−μ)), where Σ is the covariance. It deflates high-variance directions and inflates low-variance ones, reducing to Euclidean distance when Σ = I. Its constant-distance contours are ellipses aligned with the data cloud.

The cosine of the angle between two vectors, (x·y)/(‖x‖‖y‖) ∈ [−1, 1] — a measure of direction that ignores magnitude. The default for text vectors and dense embeddings; on L2-normalized vectors it ranks identically to Euclidean distance.

Set overlap, |A ∩ B| / |A ∪ B|, ranging from 0 (disjoint) to 1 (identical). The matching Jaccard distance 1−J is a true metric. It ignores shared absences, making it ideal for sparse binary or set data and near-duplicate detection (via MinHash at scale).

The family of pathologies that appear as dimension grows: neighborhoods stop being local, and pairwise distances concentrate so the nearest and farthest points become nearly equidistant. It quietly breaks k-NN and indexing on raw high-dimensional data.

The phenomenon, for data with independent coordinates, where the relative contrast (max−min)/min between pairwise distances tends to zero as dimension grows (Beyer et al., 1999). Lower-order norms such as Manhattan concentrate more slowly than Euclidean.

refs.cite: Mahalanobis, P. C. (1936, repr. 2018). On the generalised distance in statistics. Sankhyā A, 80(S1), 1–7.
Aggarwal, C. C., Hinneburg, A. & Keim, D. A. (2001). On the Surprising Behavior of Distance Metrics in High Dimensional Space. ICDT 2001, LNCS 1973, 420–434.
Beyer, K., Goldstein, J., Ramakrishnan, R. & Shaft, U. (1999). When Is "Nearest Neighbor" Meaningful? ICDT 1999, LNCS 1540, 217–235.
Cover, T. & Hart, P. (1967). Nearest neighbor pattern classification. IEEE Trans. Information Theory, 13(1), 21–27.
Broder, A. Z. (1997). On the resemblance and containment of documents. Proc. Compression and Complexity of Sequences (SEQUENCES '97).
